Author: Homer
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: αὐτίκ' ἄρ' εἰς οἶνον βάλε φάρμακον, ἔνθεν ἔπινον, νηπενθές τ' ἄχολόν τε
English_Translation_or_Summary: She cast into the wine a pharmakon: nepenthes free from gall, a remedy for grief
Ritual_Context: None — domestic/social
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Suppresses grief and memory of death; forgetting = symbolic death of self
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — pharmacological background; Egypt as drug source (parallel to Exodus/Moses/Jesus/Egypt)

Author: Homer
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: δῶκε κυκεῶ τε καὶ τυρόν... φάρμακα λύγρ'
English_Translation_or_Summary: Circe gave them kykeon with baneful pharmaka mixed in Pramnian wine
Ritual_Context: Sorcery / divine intervention
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Transformation = death of human identity; Hermes provides antidote
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — primal death-inducer/antidote dyad; kykeon = Eleusinian drink; first appearance

Author: Homer
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἀντίφαρμακον ὄπασσε... μῶλυ δέ μιν καλέουσι θεοί
English_Translation_or_Summary: Hermes gave an antidote (ἀντίφαρμακον); the gods call it moly
Ritual_Context: Divine gift for protection against death-drug
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Antidote to Circe's death/transformation; first use of ἀντίφαρμακον
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — death-inducer/antidote dyad is the primal pharmacological structure; recurs in theriac tradition

Author: Homeric Hymns
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: κυκεῶ τεῦξαι ἐκέλευσε ἀλφίτῳ ἐν ὕδατι βληχῶνί τε μαλακῷ
English_Translation_or_Summary: She commanded a kykeon of barley, water, and soft pennyroyal
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Hordeum vulgare; Mentha pulegium; Claviceps purpurea (hypothetical)
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian Mystery initiatory drink
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Demeter refuses wine (Dionysiac); accepts kykeon = initiatory substance for death/rebirth experience
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H3 — Eleusinian initiation is death/rebirth structured; kykeon is the pharmacological vehicle

Author: Pindar
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ὁ δὲ καὶ τὸν ἐκεῖθεν ἀμαχανίᾳ δεδεμένον ἤδη τιν' ἄνδρ' ἐκ θανάτου κόμισεν
English_Translation_or_Summary: Asclepius brought back a man from death — Zeus destroyed him for it
Ritual_Context: Medical-divine healing; raising the dead
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Healer who raises dead is killed = pharmacological resurrection as divine transgression; parallel to Jesus (raises Lazarus; killed)
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — Asclepius/Jesus structural parallel: pharmacological resurrection leads to healer's death

Author: Pindar
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: πολυφάρμακοι Μήδειαν... θελξίμβροτοι ἐπᾳοιδαί
English_Translation_or_Summary: Medea skilled in many drugs with man-enchanting chants
Ritual_Context: Hecate worship / Colchian magical tradition
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug + incantation inseparable; Medea = ur-pharmacist
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — mythological charter of Greek pharmacology; polypharmakos as honorific

Author: Euripides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἐκ τῆς γὰρ Ἑκάτης Φεραίας, ἣν ἐγὼ πάντων μάλιστα δαιμόνων σέβω ξύνεργον
English_Translation_or_Summary: From Hecate of Pherae whom I revere above all divine powers as my collaborator
Ritual_Context: Hecate worship / chthonic religion
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Hecate = goddess of underworld pharmaka; source of death and antidote knowledge
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — Hecate as divine source of pharmacological power; transmitted to mortal practitioners

Author: Euripides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] The poisoned robe adheres and burns; Glauce and Creon die in agony
English_Translation_or_Summary: Poisoned robe / contact poison (aconite in oil?)
Ritual_Context: Anointing garment = death by χρίω; inversion of royal consecration
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H3 — pharmacological inversion of χριστός: anointing garment kills instead of consecrates

Author: Euripides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ὁ δ' αὖ θεὸς Βρόμιος οἶνον ἀνθρώποις φέρει
English_Translation_or_Summary: The god Bromios brings wine to men; if wine dies there is no more love
Ritual_Context: Dionysiac thiasos / initiatory frenzy
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Wine = life-giver and death-inducer; sparagmos = death; ecstatic return = rebirth
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H2 — Dionysiac wine-death-rebirth structure is direct parallel to Last Supper wine theology

Author: Euripides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Agave tears off Pentheus's head in Dionysiac frenzy — sparagmos
English_Translation_or_Summary: Possession-induced ritual violence
Ritual_Context: Tearing apart (σπαραγμός) = ritual death; mirrors Dionysus's own dismemberment and resurrection
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 — sparagmos/omophagia as death-rite; resurrection of Dionysus as mystery pattern

Author: Sophocles
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Nessus claims his blood is a love potion; it is contact poison (Hydra venom)
English_Translation_or_Summary: Hydra venom / Nessus's blood (presented as φίλτρον)
Ritual_Context: Poison disguised as love-charm; no antidote = death; Heracles burns alive
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 + H3 — φίλτρον / φάρμακον reversal; death-anointing vs. life-anointing dialectic

Author: Herodotus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: σπέρματα τῆς κανναβίδος ἐπιβάλλουσι τοῖσι διαπύροισι λίθοισι... ὀλολύζουσι
English_Translation_or_Summary: They throw hemp seeds onto red-hot stones; howl with delight
Ritual_Context: Scythian funerary purification rite
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Psychoactive substance used in funerary rites; ecstatic cry = ritual response
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H3 — EARLIEST documented psychoactive ritual in Western lit; funerary context; cannabis in adjacent culture to kaneh-bosem Israelite anointing oil hypothesis

Author: Herodotus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Enarees divine using willow and linden bark (combined with cannabis)
English_Translation_or_Summary: Cannabis (combined with Hist. 4.73-75)
Ritual_Context: Divination = accessing death-knowledge; substance enables divine contact
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 — cannabis in Scythian religious system; divinatory + funerary + purificatory = complete religio-pharmacological system

Author: Herodotus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Cyrus deploys unwatered wine as military weapon to incapacitate Massagetae
English_Translation_or_Summary: Wine (ἄκρητος οἶνος — undiluted)
Ritual_Context: Wine as incapacitating pharmakon = pharmacological warfare
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 — wine as weapon illustrates pharmacological thinking in Herodotean world

Author: Plato
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Socrates drinks hemlock; numbness ascends from feet to heart; dies saying 'I owe a cock to Asclepius'
English_Translation_or_Summary: Hemlock (κώνειον)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Philosophical initiation: death as practice (μελέτη θανάτου)
Ritual_Context: Death by pharmakon as philosophical/mystical initiation; debt to Asclepius = healing-god; dying = being healed
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 + H2 — Socratic death as philosophical-pharmacological initiation; parallel to Jesus's voluntary death as spiritual act; both die in view of others as witnesses

Author: Plato
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: τὸ φύλλον δύναμίν τινα εἶχεν, ἀλλὰ χωρὶς τῆς ἐπᾠδῆς οὐδὲν ὄφελος
English_Translation_or_Summary: The leaf had power, but without the charm it was of no use
Ritual_Context: Medical-magical unity
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug + ritual incantation = complete pharmacological act
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — drug alone insufficient; ritual framing essential; parallel to Jesus's healing with word + touch + anointing

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: τρεῖς κύκλους ξίφει περιγράψαντα δεῖν ἐκτεμεῖν πρὸς ἑσπέραν ὁρῶντα
English_Translation_or_Summary: Must cut [mandrake] facing west after drawing three circles with a sword
Ritual_Context: Rhizotomoi harvest ritual: circles; west (death-direction); circumambulation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Ritual harvesting = pharmacological act is simultaneously religious act; west = realm of dead
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — pharmacological practice IS religious practice; the substances are sacred

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: δίδοσθαι δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τοῦ ἀνωδύνου τοῖς τεμνομένοις ἢ καιομένοις
English_Translation_or_Summary: Mandrake given as anesthetic to those being cut or cauterized
Ritual_Context: Surgical anesthesia
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: First description of surgical anesthesia; death-like sleep → procedure → awakening = resurrection structure
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H2 — mandrake wine as anesthetic establishes death-sleep/revival pharmacological model; relevant to Passion sponge

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: λέγουσι δ' ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ Κερβέρου στόματος ἐρρύη τὸ ἀκόνιτον
English_Translation_or_Summary: Aconite flowed from the mouth of Cerberus when Heracles dragged him from Hades
Ritual_Context: Chthonic mythological origin; Hecate's substance
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug originates in the underworld; aconite = death in plant form
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — drug mythology locates substance at boundary of life and death

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: τοῖς δὲ χρωμένοις ἐπὶ θανάτῳ σήσαμον καὶ οἶνον προστιθέασιν ὅπως βραδύτερον ἀποθνῄσκωσιν
English_Translation_or_Summary: Those using [aconite] for death add sesame and wine to slow the dying
Ritual_Context: Execution / poisoning
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Explicit dose-timing pharmacology for death management
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — death-timing pharmacology; conscious management of dying process; relevant to Passion chronology

Author: Apollonius Rhodius
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: νυκτὶ δ' ἐν ὀρφναίῃ φάρμακον ἔντυεν... ἄνθος ἐρυθρόν
English_Translation_or_Summary: In the dark of night she prepared the drug they call Promethean; its flower blood-red
Substance_Described: Promethean drug (from Prometheus's blood; midnight harvest)
Ritual_Context: Ritual midnight harvest (Hecate's hour); anointed on Jason's body as χρίσμα
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug applied to body surface = χρίω / anointing; makes Jason deathproof
Hypothesis_Relevance: H3 — Medea ANOINTS Jason with pharmakon; protective χρίσμα = same root as χριστός; anointed one = pharmacologically treated one

Author: Apollonius Rhodius
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Medea prepares sleep-inducing drug for the dragon guarding the Golden Fleece
English_Translation_or_Summary: Sleep-inducer (mandrake? opium? juniper?)
Ritual_Context: Dragon put to sleep (death-state) to allow ritual passage; sleep = temporary death
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H1 — pharmacological sleep as gateway to sacred object; ritual structure

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ὄπιον... ὑπνωτικόν ἐστι· πλεῖον δὲ λαμβανόμενον θανάσιμόν ἐστι
English_Translation_or_Summary: Opium is soporific; in excess it is lethal
Ritual_Context: Medical; key component of theriac antidote
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Death by opium = permanent sleep; threshold between sleep-dose and death-dose = mystery rite boundary
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H2 — Dioscorides CONTEMPORARY with early Christianity; documents all relevant substances; opium central to theriac

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ναρκωτικὴν καὶ καρωτικὴν δύναμιν ἔχει... μανίαν ποιεῖ
English_Translation_or_Summary: [Henbane] has narcotic and benumbing power; causes madness
Ritual_Context: Possible Dionysiac use; hallucinogenic-narcotic
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Madness → death spectrum; small dose = narcosis; medium = hallucination; large = death
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — anticholinergic hallucinations parallel Dionysiac frenzy; possible mystery cult substance

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: οἶνος μανδραγορίτης... τοῖς τεμνομένοις ἢ καιομένοις ὅπως ἀναίσθητοι γένωνται
English_Translation_or_Summary: Wine of mandrake given to those being cut so they may be without sensation
Ritual_Context: Surgical anesthesia; potential ritual use
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Death-like sleep → procedure → awakening; sponge delivery theoretically possible
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — mandrake wine on sponge = pharmacologically plausible mechanism for Passion sponge episode

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἀκόνιτον... λέγεται δὲ αὐτὸ ἀπὸ τοῦ Κερβέρου τοῦ ᾅδου σιέλου φύναι κατ' Ἑκάτης
English_Translation_or_Summary: Aconite said to grow from the spittle of Cerberus according to Hecate myth
Ritual_Context: Chthonic origin myth; Hecate; assassination poison
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug of underworld origin; by 1st c. CE the mythological-pharmacological framework is stable
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — mythological-pharmacological framework fully canonical by time of Jesus

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: κώνειον... ψυχρὸν τὴν δύναμιν... Ἀθηναῖοι κατεχρήσαντο
English_Translation_or_Summary: Hemlock cold in power... the Athenians used it for execution
Ritual_Context: Athenian execution; Socratic initiation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Institutionalized death by pharmakon; ascending paralysis
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — hemlock as institutional death-pharmakon; Socratic parallel to voluntary philosophical death

Author: Pliny the Elder
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: Mithridates... venena cotidie potavit praesumens remedia
English_Translation_or_Summary: Mithridates drank poisons daily, taking antidotes in advance
Ritual_Context: Royal self-immunization; implicit myth of death-immunity
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Man who cannot die: pharmacologically deathproof; structural resurrection mythology
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H2 — Mithridates as mythological prototype of death-overcoming by chemistry; Pontic origin = Medea's homeland

Author: Pliny the Elder
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: theriacam Andromachi... ab Mithridate rege institutam
English_Translation_or_Summary: The theriac of Andromachus instituted by King Mithridates
Ritual_Context: Medical prophylaxis; royal tradition
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Antidote as protection against all death by poison = pharmacological immortality claim
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — antidote tradition links Pontic pharmacology directly to 1st c. CE medical practice

Author: Galen
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἡ θηριακὴ ἀντίδοτος... Μιθριδάτης ὁ βασιλεὺς πρῶτος συνέταξεν
English_Translation_or_Summary: The theriac antidote first composed by King Mithridates
Ritual_Context: Imperial medicine; Galen prepares for Marcus Aurelius
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Universal antidote = pharmacological defense against death; explicit lineage Mithridates → Galen
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — Galen systematizes antidote tradition; theriac as death-conqueror in medicine

Author: Galen
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: τὴν ψυχὴν ἀπολλυμένην... ἡ θηριακὴ σώζει
English_Translation_or_Summary: The theriac saves the soul/life from perishing
Ritual_Context: Medical; implicit mythological framing
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Galen frames theriac in soteriological language: 'saves' (σώζει) = same verb as Christian soteriology
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — pharmacological salvation vs. spiritual salvation; same linguistic framework

Author: Plutarch
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: τὸν Ὄσιριν ἀναστῆσαι φαρμάκοις... εἰς ταὐτὸ συνάγει τοῖς Διονύσου μύθοις
English_Translation_or_Summary: Isis raised Osiris with pharmaka; Plutarch aligns this with Dionysus myths
Ritual_Context: Cross-cultural mystery religion comparison
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Isis uses PHARMAKA in resurrection of Osiris; Dionysus parallel made explicit by Plutarch
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — pharmakon + resurrection = universal mystery religion structure; documented by neutral observer

Author: Clement of Alexandria
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἐνήστευσα, ἔπιον τὸν κυκεῶνα, ἔλαβον ἐκ κίστης
English_Translation_or_Summary: I fasted; I drank the kykeon; I took from the chest
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian Mystery initiation (Clement reveals the synthema)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Initiate drinks kykeon as central act; mediates death/rebirth experience
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — Eleusinian synthema confirmed; kykeon as the initiatory substance; Clement (Christian!) reveals it as polemical

Author: Pausanias
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ὄναρ δέ μοι κεκώλυκεν ἐς τὸν ἐντὸς τείχους γράφειν χῶρον
English_Translation_or_Summary: A dream forbade me to describe the things within the sanctuary wall
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian sanctuary; initiate observes prohibition on revelation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Sanctuary secrets confirm initiatory substances were guarded knowledge
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 — the secrecy itself confirms something worth keeping secret; guards pharmacological ritual

Author: Orphic Gold Tablets (Pelinna)
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: νῦν ἔθανες καὶ νῦν ἐγένου, τρισόλβιε, ἄματι τῷδε
English_Translation_or_Summary: Now you have died and now you have been born on this same day. Tell Persephone that Bacchios himself released you
Ritual_Context: Orphic/Bacchic funerary initiation; underworld navigation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Death and rebirth on SAME DAY; Dionysus as liberator from death; direct resurrection formula
Hypothesis_Relevance: H1 + H2 — death/rebirth on same day = pharmacological timing of initiation; Dionysus = liberator = structural parallel to Christ

Author: Josephus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Josephus describes Essene initiation with mystery cult framing; three-year probation; sacred meals; ritual purification
English_Translation_or_Summary: Essene ritual food (controlled substances implied)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Essene community meals; three-year initiatory hierarchy; ritual purity
Ritual_Context: Initiation structure parallel to mystery cult; Josephus uses Greek mystery language to describe Jewish sect
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H2 — John the Baptist likely connected to Essene milieu; Jesus underwent John's initiation; Essene ≈ mystery cult structure

Author: Josephus
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἐκ μέθης... ἡ ψυχὴ ἐλευθερωθεῖσα... ἐπανέρχεται
English_Translation_or_Summary: As from drunkenness the soul, freed, returns
Ritual_Context: Eleazar's speech at Masada; philosophical death-contemplation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Soul leaves body in drunkenness = pharmacological death-simulation; return = resurrection
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — Jewish Hellenistic synthesis: Greek mystery/pharmacological language used by Jewish military leader

Author: Babylonian Talmud
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: כשמוציאין אותו ליהרג... נותנין לו קורט של לבונה בכוס של יין... ונשים יקרות שבירושלים היו מתנדבות
English_Translation_or_Summary: When one is led out to execution, he is given a grain of frankincense in a cup of wine to numb his senses... the noble women of Jerusalem donated and brought this
Ritual_Context: Jewish legal-charitable practice: institutionalized pharmacological death preparation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: JEWISH LAW mandated narcotic wine before execution; Jesus was offered this (Mark 15:23) and REFUSED
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — CRITICAL EVIDENCE: Jewish execution practice was pharmacological; Jesus's refusal was deliberate conscious choice; not a mystery cult import but indigenous Jewish pharmacological tradition

Author: Mark
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: καὶ ἐδίδουν αὐτῷ ἐσμυρνισμένον οἶνον· ὃς δὲ οὐκ ἔλαβεν
English_Translation_or_Summary: They offered him myrrh-mixed wine; but he did not take it
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion; parallels Talmudic death-narcotic tradition
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Jesus REFUSES the death-analgesic preparation = deliberate pharmacological choice
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — refusal of Talmudic death-narcotic shows Jesus was pharmacologically aware and made a conscious decision

Author: Matthew
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἔδωκαν αὐτῷ πιεῖν οἶνον μετὰ χολῆς μεμιγμένον· καὶ γευσάμενος οὐκ ἤθελεν πίειν
English_Translation_or_Summary: Wine with gall offered; having tasted he refused
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion preparation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Tasting then refusing: recognized and rejected the pharmacological preparation
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — two death-pharmaka offered and refused; sequence shows conscious pharmacological literacy

Author: John
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: σκεῦος ἔκειτο ὄξους μεστόν· σπόγγον οὖν μεστὸν τοῦ ὄξους ὑσσώπῳ περιθέντες
English_Translation_or_Summary: A vessel full of sour wine; sponge on hyssop branch brought to his mouth; he receives it and dies
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Acetum; possibly spiked with mandrake or opium
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion; liturgically freighted (hyssop = LXX purification plant: Ps 51:7; Ex 12:22)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Jesus accepts ὄξος → immediately dies; in sequence: refuse A → refuse B → accept C → die
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — pharmacological death sequence; sponge delivery mechanism consistent with mandrake wine; hyssop = LXX purification = ritual framing

Author: John
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἦλθεν δὲ καὶ Νικόδημος... μίγμα σμύρνης καὶ ἀλόης ὡς λίτρας ἑκατόν
English_Translation_or_Summary: Nicodemus came bringing myrrh and aloe mixture, about 100 litrai (~33 kg)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Commiphora myrrha + Aloe vera / Aquilaria agallocha
Ritual_Context: Jewish burial preparation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Massive pharmacologically active substances at burial; quantity far exceeds normal embalming
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — extraordinary quantity of pharmaka at burial; aloe may be Aquilaria (narcotic/sedative properties)

Author: Mark
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ τέκτων, ὁ υἱὸς Μαρίας
English_Translation_or_Summary: Is not this the craftsman (τέκτων), the son of Mary?
Ritual_Context: None
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Jesus identified as τέκτων (craftsman/builder) from Nazareth — 4 miles from Sepphoris (Greco-Roman city)
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — geographical argument: τέκτων in Hellenized Galilee would have worked in Greco-Roman urban environment; exposure to mystery culture virtually certain

Author: Mark
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [prose] woman anoints Jesus's head with pure nard (νάρδος πιστική) worth 300 denarii
English_Translation_or_Summary: Spikenard (νάρδος πιστική)
Ritual_Context: Pharmacologically active anointing immediately before death; Jesus anointed with narcotic-sedative plant
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: H3 — Jesus physically anointed with pharmacologically active nard before his passion; the χριστός is literally smeared with active substance

Author: Mark
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: καὶ δαιμόνια πολλὰ ἐξέβαλλον, καὶ ἤλειφον ἐλαίῳ πολλοὺς ἀρρώστους καὶ ἐθεράπευον
English_Translation_or_Summary: They cast out many demons and anointed with oil many sick and healed them
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Olive oil + active additives (possibly Exodus 30:22-25 formula)
Ritual_Context: Healing ministry; anointing as physical healing act
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Anointing oil = active pharmacological healing agent; not symbolic; disciples routinely anointed sick
Hypothesis_Relevance: H3 — early Christian healing practice was physical anointing with oil; directly continuous with OT anointing tradition

Author: James
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: εὐχέτωσαν ἐπ' αὐτὸν ἀλείψαντες ἐλαίῳ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ κυρίου
English_Translation_or_Summary: Let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Olive oil (+ active additives assumed from OT prescription)
Ritual_Context: Church healing practice; elders anoint sick
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Physical anointing with oil as active healing sacrament; not symbolic gesture
Hypothesis_Relevance: H3 — anointing in early church was physical healing practice; continuous from χριστός etymology through institutional practice

Author: Paul
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: ἢ ἀγνοεῖτε ὅτι ὅσοι ἐβαπτίσθημεν εἰς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν, εἰς τὸν θάνατον αὐτοῦ ἐβαπτίσθημεν;
English_Translation_or_Summary: Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?
Ritual_Context: Christian baptism as death/rebirth initiation rite
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Paul EXPLICITLY frames baptism as death; burial with Christ; resurrection to new life — mystery structure stated plainly
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — Paul's baptism theology is mystery initiation theology; death-submersion = mystery initiation = John's rite

Author: Paul
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: συνερχομένων ὑμῶν ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό, οὐκ ἔστιν κυριακὸν δεῖπνον φαγεῖν. ἕκαστος γὰρ τὸ ἴδιον δεῖπνον προλαμβάνει ἐν τῷ φαγεῖν, καὶ ὃς μὲν πεινᾷ, ὃς δὲ μεθύει
English_Translation_or_Summary: When you come together it is not the Lord's supper you eat; one goes hungry another gets drunk (μεθύει)
Ritual_Context: Eucharistic meal; early Corinthian practice
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Corinthian Christians are becoming PHYSICALLY INTOXICATED at the Eucharist; pharmacological effect not metaphorical
Hypothesis_Relevance: H2 — early Christian Eucharist involved enough wine to produce intoxication; mystery cult parallels documented by Justin Martyr and Julian

Author: Justin Martyr
Greek_or_Source_Text_Excerpt: [Greek/Latin] The wicked devils imitated in the mysteries of Mithras commanding the same thing to be done [bread and cup]
English_Translation_or_Summary: N/A — polemical comparison
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Justin comparing Christian Eucharist to Mithraic mystery meal
